heading: OTE AL AGGIO INTRODUTTIVO
card_summary: This chunk comprises the scholarly endnotes (notes 1–48) to Saverio Campanini's introductory essay to the Bompiani edition of Zorzi's De Harmonia Mundi. The notes document the genesis of Campanini's research (originating as a 1999 Turin doctoral thesis), correct Zorzi's birth date using an astrological nativity chart at the Bodleian Library (ms. Canon. Misc. 24), and cite the major bibliographic authorities on Zorzi's life and work (Vasoli, Degli Agostini, Vicentini). They record Zorzi's personal kabbalistic library, his encounter with Agrippa of Nettesheim in Padua, his role in the Henry VIII divorce proceedings, his connections to Chiara Bugni and Venetian religious milieu, and his calendar calculations predicting the world's end in 2239 CE. A crucial document is the extended Latin letter from Agrippa describing his brief meeting with Zorzi and Zorzi's promise to share his Hebrew books, including a Kabbalah of Samuel (attributed to Shemu'el ibn Motot or ibn Zarza).
essay:
# Notes to the Introductory Essay (pp. 123–127)

## Nature and Scope of This Chunk

Chunk 27 consists entirely of the numbered endnotes (1–48) to Campanini's *saggio introduttivo* (introductory essay) to his Italian edition of Francesco Zorzi's *De Harmonia Mundi* (Bompiani, 2010). These notes are dense with archival references, manuscript sigla, bibliographic citations, and short but significant quotations from primary sources. They function as the documentary apparatus for claims made in the body of the introduction and collectively constitute a substantial resource for the reconstruction of Zorzi's intellectual biography.

## Campanini's Scholarly Biography (Note 1)

Note 1 establishes the provenance and intellectual journey of the edition itself:

> Il lavoro che segue rappresenta una versione alquanto ampliata, aggiornata e sotto molti punti di vista migliorata della tesi di dottorato che ho presentato nel 1999 presso l'Università degli Studi di Torino.

<cite>p. 123, note 1</cite>

Campanini traces his decade-long scholarly pilgrimage from Ca' Foscari (Venice) through Oxford's Centre for Hebrew and Jewish Studies, the Freie Universität Berlin (Institut für Judaistik and Institut für Philosophie), the Alma Mater in Bologna, the Dipartimento di Ravenna, and finally the IRHT-CNRS in Paris. This itinerary reflects the interdisciplinary demands of the project — requiring Hebrew philology, philosophy, and codicology.

## Zorzi's Birth Date and Horoscope (Note 2)

Note 2 corrects an erroneous date of 1460 that had circulated in previous scholarship. The correction is grounded in a *tema natale* (astrological nativity) drawn up by the physician and astrologer Antonio Gazio, preserved at the Bodleian Library, Oxford (ms. Canon. Misc. 24, fol. 42r). The discovery is attributed to J.-F. Maillard (MAILLARD 1991, p. 230, n. 33). This is a significant archival find, as it anchors Zorzi's biography to a precise document.

## Bibliographic Infrastructure for Zorzi's Life (Note 3)

The principal sources for Zorzi's biography are listed: Vasoli (1974; 2005), Degli Agostini (1754), Berengo Morte (1946), and Vicentini (1952; 1954–1957). The work by Lepori (1967) was inaccessible to Campanini.

## Citation System for De Harmonia Mundi (Note 4)

Note 4 explains the notation system used throughout the edition: citations to the *De Harmonia Mundi* reproduce cantico (canticle), tono (tone), and capitolo (chapter). For the eighth tone of the third canticle, a four-index system applies: canticle, tone, modulo, and chapter. This is a key reference for any reader navigating the apparatus.

## Chiara Bugni and Mystical Experience (Notes 11–13)

Notes 11–13 concern the visionary Franciscan tertiary Chiara Bugni, whose experiences Zorzi cites as contemporary confirmation of mystical states comparable to those of Clare of Assisi. Note 12 quotes Zorzi's own Latin testimony:

> Clara Assisias a coena Domini iacens usque ad resurrectionis diem, crucis trophaeum altius contemplans, dum reversa est, visa sibi fuit nec horam quidem acquievisse. Quod comprobavi in altera, nomine et vita consimili, quae assumpto sacramento in crepuscolo eiusdem quintae feriae, genibus innixa stetit immobilis usque ad diem Dominicum, quo veluti cum Christo resurgens, multa mihi enarravit, quae viderat.

<cite>p. 123, note 12, citing De harmonia mundi 3,4,4</cite>

Note 13 connects Chiara Bugni's case to the later Venetian episode of Guillaume Postel (REDPATH 1985), and to Zarri's scholarship on holy women (ZARRI 1980).

## Calendar and Eschatological Calculations (Notes 19, 21, 25–26)

Several notes bear on Zorzi's computus and eschatological thinking. Note 19 specifies that the end Zorzi computes is not imminent: his calculations place the annus mundi 6000 at 2239 CE. Note 21 quotes Zorzi's own dating of his composition to 1523:

> [...] nunc quando degimus annum a Christi nativitate supputatum, Millesimum Quingentesimum vigesimum tertium.

<cite>p. 124, note 21, citing De harmonia mundi 3,7,6</cite>

Note 26 provides a key passage on Zorzi's astronomical observation of the precession of the equinoxes — the sun's entry into Capricorn has shifted from December 25 to December 11 over 1520 years, at a rate of approximately one day per century:

> Nos autem comprobatum habemus ab anno nativitatis Christi, quo Sol vigesimoquinto decembris die intrabat in Capricornum hucusque, quo intrat die undecimo, vel circa, Solem in 1520 annis praevenisse suum introitum quatuordecim diebus, vel circa: unde clara luce comprobatur, quod quibuslibet centum annis praeveniat per diem in quo solet moveri gradu uno.

<cite>p. 125, note 26, citing De harmonia mundi 1,6,30</cite>

This connects Zorzi's harmonics to the tradition of calendar reform that would culminate in the Gregorian reform of 1582, which Zorzi anticipates (note 25 references his *Problemata* calculation estimating 16–18 surplus days in the Julian calendar).

## Zorzi's Kabbalistic Library and the Henry VIII Affair (Notes 29–37)

Notes 29–38 address two related matters: Zorzi's role in the Henry VIII annulment controversy and his personal kabbalistic library. Note 29 cites from the *Problemata* Zorzi's claim to have personally facilitated the conversion of a prominent rabbi. Note 34 raises the question of whether Zorzi's canonical opinions on the divorce — believed lost — might survive in ms. Piancastelli VII 57 at the Biblioteca comunale di Forlì. Notes 35–36 cite the scriptural basis of Zorzi's argument (Matt. 19:6; Mark 10:9) and a key passage from the *Problemata*:

> Nam Salvator ait: quos Deus coniunxit, homo non separet. Et si aliqui separantur per divortium, forsitan illos Deus non coniunxerat.

<cite>p. 125, note 36, citing Problemata 2,7,374</cite>

## Agrippa's Testimony on Zorzi's Hebrew Books (Note 39)

Note 39 is among the most significant in the chunk. It reproduces the extended Latin account by Heinrich Cornelius Agrippa of Nettesheim describing his meeting with Zorzi in Padua, from Agrippa's *Opera* (Lyon, vol. II, pp. 1030–1033). The passage reveals Zorzi's possession of rare Hebrew kabbalistic manuscripts and his willingness — with limits — to share them:

> [...] me in itinere meo Italico Patavii convenisse Patrem Franciscum Georgium Venetum, qui librum de Harmonia mundi conscripsit, qui cum iturus esset Venetias, utile mihi esse ducebam, cum eo me Venetias conferre, eumque alloqui, cuius humanitate, brevissimo colloquio, eoque ex itinere ac pene furtivo vix mihi perfrui licuit per quartam horam partem [...] Illud et quidem affirmavit eos hebraeos libros quos et tu et ego tantopere et cupimus et perquirimus, apud se esse, neque eos mihi negaturum, verum legendos, non autem excribendos [...] Cabala autem Samuelis mihi promissa fuerat, sed frater ille Georgius dixit, me in ea parum posse proficere.

<cite>pp. 126–127, note 39</cite>

The "Cabala of Samuel" mentioned at the close is glossed in note 38 as possibly the commentary on *Sefer Yetzirah* by Shemu'el ibn Motot, or alternatively the super-commentary by Shemu'el ibn Zarza on Abraham ibn Ezra's Torah commentary — a text Campanini believes Zorzi used without explicitly listing it in his own catalogue of kabbalistic sources.

## Egidio da Viterbo and the Kabbalistic Network (Note 40)

Note 40 widens the circle of Christian kabbalists seeking access to Zorzi's library. In a letter of 14 December 1513 (Ms. Angelica 688, fol. 48), Egidio da Viterbo wrote requesting a summary catalogue of the Hebrew books owned by "el frate di S. Francesco." This triangulation — Agrippa, Egidio, and Zorzi — maps the northern Italian Christian kabbalist network of the early sixteenth century.

## Connections to Zorzi's Larger Project

These notes collectively illuminate the material and social conditions of Zorzi's *De Harmonia Mundi*: his access to a remarkable private kabbalistic library, his participation in live controversies (the Venetian reform milieu, the Henry VIII affair), his empirical attention to astronomical data (calendar reform, precession), and his personal acquaintance with visionary figures (Chiara Bugni). The notes also establish the genealogy of Campanini's edition itself, situating it within a decade-long European scholarly collaboration and correction of prior errors in the received biographical tradition.
